title: Assessment: Dynamic Programming — The Three-Stage Pipeline

questions.question: You see an optimisation problem that asks for the maximum profit from buying and selling stock. Which two properties would confirm it is solvable with DP?

options.A: The array is sorted and has no duplicates
options.B: Optimal substructure: optimal solution uses optimal sub-decisions; overlapping subproblems: same sub-computations recur
options.C: The problem involves a graph with weighted edges
options.D: The answer is a single integer (not a sequence), indicating DP over enumeration

questions.explanation: DP requires both properties. Optimal substructure: maximum profit on days 1..i depends on optimal decisions on days 1..i-1. Overlapping subproblems: without caching, the naive recursion recomputes the same sub-results. A sorted array or integer answer does not by itself indicate DP.

questions.question: Adding `@functools.cache` to a correct recursive function converts it to memoised DP. What is the time complexity of the memoised Fibonacci using this approach?

options.A: O(2^n) — the same as the non-memoised version
options.B: O(n) — each Fibonacci number is computed exactly once and cached
options.C: O(n log n) — cache lookup adds log n overhead per call
options.D: O(1) — the cache stores all results after the first full run

questions.explanation: fib(n) without caching has O(2^n) calls (exponential branching). With caching, each unique argument n is computed exactly once. There are exactly n+1 unique arguments (0..n). Total time: O(n) calls × O(1) per call = O(n).

questions.question: You are implementing 0/1 Knapsack in 1D DP. The inner loop iterates `for cap in range(W, weight-1, -1)` in reverse. What happens if you iterate forward instead?

options.A: The result is the same — forward vs reverse makes no difference for 0/1 knapsack
options.B: Items can be used multiple times (unbounded knapsack behaviour) because earlier updates pollute later lookups in the same row
options.C: The algorithm produces an off-by-one error on the boundary condition
options.D: The algorithm runs faster due to better cache locality

questions.explanation: In 1D DP, dp[cap] = max(dp[cap], dp[cap-weight]+value). Forward iteration: when computing dp[cap], dp[cap-weight] may already reflect the current item being added this iteration → item counted twice (unbounded knapsack). Reverse ensures dp[cap-weight] is from the previous item's state.

questions.question: Coin Change problem: given coins=[1,3,4] and amount=6. Greedy (always take largest coin ≤ remaining) takes: 4+1+1=3 coins. What does DP find?

options.A: 3 coins — same as greedy, confirming greedy is optimal
options.B: 2 coins: 3+3=2 — DP finds the global optimum that greedy misses
options.C: 1 coin: 6=6 — DP finds a denomination that greedy doesn't see
options.D: 4 coins — greedy was better; DP is suboptimal for coin change

questions.explanation: DP recurrence: dp[0]=0, dp[v] = min(dp[v-c]+1) for each coin c. dp[6] via paths: dp[3]+1=2 (using coin 3 twice). Greedy takes 4 first, leaving 2, then 1+1. DP finds 3+3=2 coins. This is the canonical counterexample showing greedy fails on non-canonical coin sets.

questions.question: You need to optimise dp space for Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) from O(m×n) to O(min(m,n)). What property of the recurrence makes this possible?

options.A: LCS only depends on adjacent characters, so two variables suffice
options.B: LCS dp[i][j] depends only on dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], and dp[i][j-1] — only the current and previous row needed
options.C: LCS can be solved greedily in O(n) without DP
options.D: LCS values are monotonically increasing, allowing binary search over the table

questions.explanation: LCS recurrence: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1 if match, else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Only row i-1 is needed to compute row i. Keep two 1D arrays (or one with careful update order), reducing space from O(m×n) to O(n) (or O(min(m,n)) by iterating over the shorter string).

questions.question: For Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown, a 1D dp[i] representing max profit on day i is insufficient. What additional dimension is needed and why?

options.A: dp[i][price] — track the current stock price to make the sell decision
options.B: dp[i][state] where state ∈ {holding, sold, rest} — profit depends on which state you are in on that day
options.C: dp[i][transactions] — number of transactions alters what actions are available
options.D: dp[i][cash] — cash balance determines whether you can buy

questions.explanation: After selling (sold state), you must wait one day (rest state) before buying again (→ holding state). The available transitions differ per state: you can only buy when in rest, sell when holding, etc. 1D dp[i] can't distinguish these — you need dp[i][state].

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of top-down memoisation over bottom-up tabulation?

options.B: Top-down only computes subproblems that are actually needed; some subproblems may never be reached
options.C: Top-down is always faster due to hash map caching vs array access
options.D: Top-down avoids the need to identify base cases

questions.explanation: Top-down is lazy: it only computes a subproblem when first accessed. If the optimal solution requires only a fraction of all subproblems, top-down avoids the rest. Bottom-up fills the entire table even if most entries are unused. This matters for sparse problems like game trees.

questions.question: Write the memoised top-down Fibonacci function in Python using @cache. Then state its time complexity, space complexity (including call stack), and the base cases.

required_points: def fib(n): if n <= 1: return n; return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
Time: O(n) — each n computed once
Space: O(n) auxiliary for cache + O(n) call stack = O(n) total
answer_key.acceptable_variations: @lru_cache(maxsize=None) also accepted; base cases may include fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1 or fib(0)=1, fib(1)=1 (Fibonacci variant noted)
answer_key.full_credit: Correct code with @cache, correct base cases, correct complexities for both time and space
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct code but missing complexity analysis or incorrect complexity

questions.question: Describe the three-stage DP problem-solving pipeline (recognition, model, implement) and explain what happens if you skip the middle stage and go directly from recognition to implementation.

required_points: Stage 1 Recognition: identify optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems
Stage 2 Model: define dp[...] precisely, write the recurrence, identify base cases, determine evaluation order
Stage 3 Implement: code the recurrence (either memoised or tabulated)

Skipping Stage 2: without a precise state definition and recurrence, code becomes trial-and-error. Common failure: wrong state dimension, missing cases, incorrect base case → wrong answer with difficult-to-debug logic
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Stage names may vary; emphasise the necessity of state definition before coding
answer_key.full_credit: All three stages described accurately, consequence of skipping Stage 2 explained

questions.prompt: A colleague always writes tabulation (bottom-up) DP and never uses memoisation, arguing it is always faster. Evaluate this claim. When is tabulation preferable, when is memoisation preferable, and what property of the problem determines the choice?
questions.guidance: Strong answer: tabulation iterative = no call stack, easier space optimisation (rolling array), cache-friendly array access. Memoisation = only compute needed subproblems, natural recursion structure matches problem. Key property: if all states must be computed (dense subproblem graph), tabulation wins. If many states never reached (sparse subproblem graph), memoisation wins.

rubric.A: Evaluates both approaches with specific advantages, identifies the dense/sparse subproblem property as the deciding factor with an example
rubric.B: Correctly describes when each is better but uses vague criteria instead of the density of subproblem access
rubric.C: Describes one approach as universally better without engaging the tradeoff
